overview.title: FunncyDream Ransomware Attack on Healthcare Provider
overview.description: The FunnyDream campaign targets a healthcare provider through a multi-stage cyber attack starting with a phishing email, leading to ransomware deployment. The attack leverages known vulnerabilities and weak configurations to infiltrate the network, escalate privileges, and disrupt operations.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2018-0167 (CWE-502): Adobe Flash Player Uninitialized Memory Stack-Based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability
stage.description: Attackers send phishing emails containing malicious links to healthcare staff, exploiting CVE-2018-0167 via compromised websites. Employees click these links, granting initial access to the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2023-22518 (CWE-601): Improper Neutralization of CRLF in web application input fields
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 253 Name: Remote Code Inclusion
stage.description: Attackers exploit a web application vulnerability (CVE-2023-22518) to execute arbitrary code, establishing a foothold in the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 70 Name: Try Common or Default Usernames and Passwords
stage.description: Attackers create a new user account with administrative privileges on the compromised web server to maintain persistence.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 635 Name: Alternative Execution Due to Deceptive Filenames
stage.description: Exploiting LFI vulnerabilities to access sensitive files and escalate privileges within the healthcare network.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malware modifies system logs to erase evidence of unauthorized access, covering tracks for further malicious activities.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Ransomware encrypts critical patient data, disrupting operations and demanding payment for decryption keys, causing significant financial and operational harm.

Scenario.summary: The FunncyDream campaign successfully compromises a healthcare provider through a series of well-coordinated steps, from initial phishing to ransomware deployment, highlighting vulnerabilities in web applications and the importance of robust incident response plans.
